item.title: Bait and Switch Title
item.content:encoded: How did you get that title. is it after your page has received enough votes

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded: I offered the above critique based upon my understanding of several fundamental basics of how I have observed the site to operate. I apologize if my critique came off in any way offensive or hostile, that was never my intent, and I can see several places that I could have certainly chosen my words better. That being said, it appears that we have different opinions on those same fundamental basics, and I see now that my critique isn't as helpful as I intended it to be. As such, I shall politely withdraw and change to a novote. For what it's worth, the parts that I did enjoy, I really enjoyed, and I hope that this article finds success.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded:
That's not how classification usually works. It's not solely one thing or solely another, it's a mixture of ease of containment and threat to human life.

In my experience, the authors I've spoken with don't consider threat to human life when they choose an object class. My understanding is that threat factored into an older version of the object class system that's since been antiquated as the site gets more and more harmless Keters and dangerous Safes.

If that's how your Object Classes work, totally valid, don't have a problem with it; but I don't think the majority of modern authors use them the same way.

+ Also…
– hide block

If you're having trouble figuring things out, I'd suggest looking at some of the other comments, which may offer some insight.

Oh? Is that support for Elogee's wire theory?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

But as the author, you need to ensure you're getting your ideas across in a succinct and understandable way. I was confused as to what was really going on for a lot of this.

See, this is fair criticism, but it's entirely separate from your original approach, which criticized aspects of the article that contradicted your theory. The former is helpful, the latter less so.

Often part of the fun of articles like this is community engagement. If you're having trouble figuring things out, I'd suggest looking at some of the other comments, which may offer some insight.

Classification is not dependent on how dangerous or hostile something is. It is about how difficult it is to contain. If, until it is neutralized, they have no way to truly contain it, it's Keter.

That's not how classification usually works. It's not solely one thing or solely another, it's a mixture of ease of containment and threat to human life. As an anomaly that is not physically contained but poses no apparent threat of affecting normalcy or harming people (i.e. actual containment is easy), it falls firmly into the euclid category. Maybe this is your headcanon, but that's not how it's usually written.

And you'd be logged in with the same credentials the whole time.

Clearly the way the article is written shows that this is not the case. Is it really so beyond belief that the in-fiction system might work this way that it creates a massive problem for your immersion? Obviously if that is how you approach articles I can't really change it, but it sounds like a poor barrier to enjoyment.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded:
As far as interpretation goes, yes, some of that lies on the reader. But as the author, you need to ensure you're getting your ideas across in a succinct and understandable way. I was confused as to what was really going on for a lot of this.

Classification is not dependent on how dangerous or hostile something is. It is about how difficult it is to contain. If, until it is neutralized, they have no way to truly contain it, it's Keter.

And I'm the same person reading the article the whole time. It doesn't make sense that I'd turn into the administrator at the end. And you'd be logged in with the same credentials the whole time.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Some of this criticism kind of rubs me the wrong way. I think this is what's bothering me:

I think the concept works, but the actions of D-Class blamed on this thing are really clashing with how I imagine the Foundation operates.

If you come up with a theory about what's going on, and then discover that the article clashes with the theory, that doesn't necessarily mean the article is contradictory. It could just mean that your theory isn't supported by the text, and that something else is going on. Several of your bullet points are explained by this. It's not a matter of the article being consistent, but there being evidence against your interpretation within the text. Perhaps a different theory aligns more closely to what is presented; that kind of experience is what mysteries like this are supposed to provoke.

Other assorted points:

Also, should this thing not be Keter? They seem to lack any real system of containment.

As the article mentions, all hostile activity has ceased. As far as they can tell, it isn't doing anything anymore and neutralization is imminent.

In the universe of me as the reader of this article, you establish me as the Administrator, or someone with the same level of clearance.

This whole point is explained pretty simply: logging in as Administrator comes after the display of the article, and in what is indicated to be an entirely separate console. As such, it's a weird point to be such of an issue.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit Post
item.content:encoded:
This is going to contain spoilers so I'm gonna collapse it here.
Close

Hi there, I'm Doctor Fullham, and I will be giving your SCP a thorough examination. Let me get my gloves on, turn your head and cough, ah, lovely. Let's begin.

I am of the belief that an SCP article should stand on its own two feet, with a self-contained narrative, disconnected from any supplemental Tales. In service of this, I have not read the linked tale, and will not until after this review is written.

My interpretation of the article is that SCP-5077 doesn't exist, it was created by the Foundation in order to, effectively, gaslight the D-Class and hide the fact that they have more power than they think. Classic oppression tactics. They made up an anomaly that only affected D-Class and used it to effectively turn them against one another. They then isolated and killed all the D-Class involved ion the riot, and blamed the deaths on 5077.

I have a few issues with this.

  1. A riot is something that normally takes a while to organize, and in my head, Foundation sites are not prisons. Every site does not have thousands of D-Class interned there. Why were there so many at Site-61?
  2. The Foundation would probably notice the beginnings of a riot and quash it early. I imagine there aren't many, if any, places that D-Class can get to without being under complete supervision.
  3. The D-Class have some idea of the kinds of things the Foundation deals with and how MTFs and on-site security must be armed. Hell, some of them might know that there are on-site nukes. What do they think a riot is really gonna do? How did the riot get to the point that it caused a major breach?
  4. Why is the Foundation bothering doing all this when they could have just mass amnestized Site-61's D-Class well before this ever happened? I also personally hc that the Foundation probably has mood suppression drugs and things like that to make the D-Class more compliant, and the threat of great violence if that fails.
  5. If any or all of the events were made up by the O5, it's really hard to tell.

Something about the base idea just don't sit quite right. I think the concept works, but the actions of D-Class blamed on this thing are really clashing with how I imagine the Foundation operates. Also, should this thing not be Keter? They seem to lack any real system of containment.

But the biggest issue is in a combination of two lines:

Welcome, Administrator.

  • Alteration and destruction of human testing logs for SCP-████

In the universe of me as the reader of this article, you establish me as the Administrator, or someone with the same level of clearance. The Administrator. The head honcho. The big cheese. There should not be a singe shred of information that I cannot see. I am the highest clearance level that anyone at the Foundation has. I think I know why you did this. You wanted to have the feel of a cross-link but didn't want to make the mistake of a pointless cross-link. But you replaced it with a completely incongruous use of redacting information.

This may seem like a small issue, but it really shines a light on the fact that you need to remember how much access your reader has here. You could have written that first line as "destroyed human testing records for multiple anomalies," and just left it vague. But you cannot tell me I have the highest access and then hide such a relatively harmless piece of information. I was already a bit on the fence with all of the D-Class stuff, and that unfortunately tipped me to a downvote. But it was just barely over the border. With a little bit of polishing, this could easily become +1 territory.

Please let me know if you take issue or need clarification on any of the above, I am usually lurking in IRC, or you can send me a wikidot PM, though my response will likely be much slower.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Doctor Fullham or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ That sounds interesting
-

That may be why it's a bundle of wires and ribbons. Maybe the D-Class were bound to the Foundation's experiments by wires and the like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers?
– hide block

As I started reading my first thought was, oh, this thing isn't real, the D-Class just use it as an excuse for their assassination attempts/insubordination.

But then I realized, that's not really possible; D-Class probably wouldn't have access to an EMP, or be able to corrode pipes — not to mention the sheer coordination required to get their stories straight.

Then the article mentions that the Foundation can study its thaumaturgical properties to the extent that they can magically neutralized it; and, not to mention, the O5 transcript seems to discuss 5077 very seriously as a "spawn" of the collective trauma of the D-Class at Site-61 due to the particularly inhumane experiments going on.

I'm inclined to believe 5077 was real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Correct me if I’m wrong, but...
- It seems to me the D-Class are collectively gaslighting the Foundation?

Even if that isn’t the case, I really like this article. Interesting anomaly, and the image is superbly creepy. +1.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is from here, and is CC BY 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Today, TyGently learns that they have to google prospective numbers, not just search the wiki to see if they've been used before.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This article was posted as a part of the 2020 Exquisite Corpse contest. I was given the object class and containment procedures that a mysterious other user had written, and then had to write a full article with those as the beginning.

First off, thanks to my conproc donor TheBlueHour for providing the skip seed I nurtured into a mighty skip tree.

Thanks to Jekeled and tawny0 for offering critique prior to posting.

If you liked this article, check out my Author Page for more like and unlike it!

More details to come, maybe.
